title: Proposed Renewable Fuel Standards for 2026 and 2027
content:
On June 13, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) released a proposed rule for the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) that sets increased renewable volume obligations (RVOs) for 2026 and 2027, with totals of 24.02 billion and 24.46 billion Renewable Identification Numbers (RINs) respectivelyâ€”an 8% and 2% increase over the previous year. The rule also proposes changes to the program, including reducing the 2025 cellulosic biofuel RVO, eliminating electricity (eRINs) from eligibility, and restricting imported biofuels by assigning them only 50% of the RIN value compared to domestic fuels.

The rule includes detailed nested targets for cellulosic, biobased diesel, advanced, and conventional biofuels and introduces a new methodology for projecting cellulosic volumes based on actual CNG/LNG fleet use. To ensure compliance, EPA is proposing stricter recordkeeping requirements regarding feedstock origin. The rule also clarifies definitions for feedstock origins across various sources, from crop residues to municipal waste and biogas.

The proposal has received strong backing from biofuel trade groups such as the Renewable Fuels Association (RFA), Growth Energy, Clean Fuels Alliance America, and the American Coalition for Ethanol (ACE), which see it as a growth catalyst for domestic agriculture and renewable energy. These groups also urge the EPA to go further by reallocating small refinery exemptions (SREs) and adopting updated lifecycle emissions models.

However, the proposal has drawn criticism from the American Biogas Council and RNG Coalition, who argue that it undercuts the rapid growth of cellulosic fuels, particularly renewable natural gas (RNG), by slowing target growth and not aligning with actual production trends. The public comment period is open through August 8, with a virtual public hearing scheduled for July 8.
author: Analysts Account
